coredata.dc:title: Effect of non-acoustic parameters on heterogeneous sonoporation mediated by single-pulse ultrasound and microbubbles
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Peng
Xu, Lin
Han, Tao
Du, Lianfang
Yu, Alfred C.H.
coredata.dc:description: Sonoporation—transient plasma membrane perforation elicited by the interaction of ultrasound waves with microbubbles—has shown great potential for drug delivery and gene therapy. However, the heterogeneity of sonoporation introduces complexities and challenges in the realization of controllable and predictable drug delivery. The aim of this investigation was to understand how non-acoustic parameters (bubble related and bubble-cell interaction parameters) affect sonoporation. Using a customized ultrasound-exposure and fluorescence-imaging platform, we observed sonoporation dynamics at the single-cell level and quantified exogenous molecular uptake levels to characterize the degree of sonoporation. Sonovue microbubbles were introduced to passively regulate microbubble-to-cell distance and number, and bubble size. 1MHz ultrasound with 10-cycle pulse duration and 0.6MPa peak negative pressure were applied to trigger the inertial collapse of microbubbles. Our data revealed the impact of non-acoustic parameters on the heterogeneity of sonoporation. (i) The localized collapse of relatively small bubbles (diameter, D <5.5μm) led to predictable sonoporation, the degree of which depended on the bubble-to-cell distance (d). No sonoporation was observed when d/D >1, whereas reversible sonoporation occurred when d/D <1. (ii) Large bubbles (D >5.5μm) exhibited translational movement over large distances, resulting in unpredictable sonoporation. Translation towards the cell surface led to variable reversible sonoporation or irreversible sonoporation, and translation away from the cell caused either no or reversible sonoporation. (iii) The number of bubbles correlated positively with the degree of sonoporation when D <5.5μm and d/D <1. Localized collapse of two to three bubbles mainly resulted in reversible sonoporation, whereas irreversible sonoporation was more likely following the collapse of four or more bubbles. These findings offer useful insight into the relationship between non-acoustic parameters and the degree of sonoporation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Microbubbles
Acoustic cavitation
Heterogeneous sonoporation
Non-acoustic parameters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Non-acoustic parameters related to the heterogeneity of sonoporated cells
Reversible sonoporation induced by the localized collapse of small bubbles
Unpredictable sonoporation caused by the translation of large bubbles
Dependence of small-bubble-induced sonoporation on the bubble-to-cell distance
Correlation between the number of bubbles and the degree of sonoporation
Effective spatial range (d/D <1) for triggering sonoporation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the experimental procedure. The time is relative to the moment at which the ultrasound was initiated. Membrane-impermeable fluorescence marker (Propidium iodide) was used as the sonoporation tracer and Cell-permeable probe (Calcein Blue AM) was used as the fluorescence agent for the viability assays.

Overview of the ultrasound exposure and image observation setup. (A) Experimental apparatus. (B) Enlarged view of region a showing the position of the microbubbles, cells, and transducer in the customized sample holder. (C) 1D profile of the peak negative pressure at different distances from the center of the transducer. (D) 2D profile of the peak negative pressure at a distance of 8mm from the transducer. The gray ellipse represents the microscopic field of view. The sample in the field of view has a nominal peak negative pressure of 0.6MPa.

Heterogeneously sonoporated cells identified by quantifying the internalization of propidium iodide (PI). (A–E) Bright-field, PI, and Calcein Blue fluorescence images of the negative control and sonoporated cells at different times, pre- and post-ultrasound exposure. Cell 1 is the negative control (i.e., without ultrasound exposure); Cells 2–5 were exposed to a single 10-μs ultrasound pulse at 0.6-MPa peak negative pressure. The boundaries of each cell are labeled and the pre-exposure microbubble positions are indicated. D and d are the microbubble diameter and the distance between the bubble and the cell, respectively. The scale bar represents 10μm. (F) Time trace of the relative PI fluorescence for Cells 1–5 labeled in (A–E); the analysis was commenced at 0s (the beginning of ultrasound exposure) and ended at 520s in 30-s intervals.

(A) Different types of sonoporated cells characterized by quantitative and statistical analysis of the relative PI fluorescence of more than 160 sonicated cells. The number of sonicated cells was greater than 35 for each sonoporated cell type. ***Statistically significant difference (p <0.001). (B) Corresponding statistics for relative Calcein Blue AM fluorescence for viability assays of the different types of sonoporated cells. ***Statistically significant difference (p <0.001).

Influence of bubble diameter (D) on sonoporation analyzed by the relative PI fluorescence intensity for fixed bubble-to-cell distances (d) of (A) d ≈0μm; (B) d ≈1.5μm, (C) d ≈3μm, and (D) d ≈4.5μm. The blank, pink, bright red, and crimson circles denote type I, type II (sub-groups II-1 and II-2), and type III sonoporation, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Influence of bubble-to-cell distance (d) on the relative PI fluorescence intensity for fixed bubble diameters (D) of (A) D ≈2.5μm; (B) D ≈3.5μm; (C) D ≈4.5μm. Blank pink and bright red circles represent types I and type II (sub-groups II-1 and II-2) sonoporation, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Relationship between the number of bubbles and the degree of sonoporation, analyzed by quantifying the relative PI fluorescence in the sonoporated cells. The blank pink, bright red, and crimson columns represent the percentage of type I, type II (sub-groups II-1 and II-2), and type III sonoporation, respectively. The number of sonicated cells used in the statistical analysis is shown in brackets below each column. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Relationship between the normalized parameter d/D and the degree of sonoporation, analyzed by the intracellular relative PI fluorescence for bubbles with diameters (A) less than and (B) greater than 5.5μm. The blank pink, bright red, and crimson circles represent type I, type II (sub-groups II-1 and II-2), and type III sonoporation, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Summative assessment of the influence of the non-acoustic parameters investigated on the heterogeneity of sonoporation. (A) The localized collapse of small bubbles (D <5.5μm) resulted in predictable sonoporation, the degree of which correlated with d/D: (a) d/D >1; (b) d/D <1. (B) The translational motion of large bubbles (D >5.5μm) led to unpredictable sonoporation, dependent of the translational direction of the bubbles: (a) towards the cell surface; (b) away from the cell. (C) The number of bubbles positively correlated with the degree of sonoporation when D <5.5μm and d/D <1: (a) two to three bubbles; (b) four or more bubbles.

head.ce:title: Effect of non-acoustic parameters on heterogeneous sonoporation mediated by single-pulse ultrasound and microbubbles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Instrument Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Instrument Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Laboratory of Plant Molecular Genetics, CAS Center for Excellence in Molecular Plant Sciences, Institute of Plant Physiology and Ecology, Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Laboratory of Plant Molecular Genetics
CAS Center for Excellence in Molecular Plant Sciences
Institute of Plant Physiology and Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ultrasound, Shanghai First People’s Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sonoporation—transient plasma membrane perforation elicited by the interaction of ultrasound waves with microbubbles—has shown great potential for drug delivery and gene therapy. However, the heterogeneity of sonoporation introduces complexities and challenges in the realization of controllable and predictable drug delivery. The aim of this investigation was to understand how non-acoustic parameters (bubble related and bubble-cell interaction parameters) affect sonoporation. Using a customized ultrasound-exposure and fluorescence-imaging platform, we observed sonoporation dynamics at the single-cell level and quantified exogenous molecular uptake levels to characterize the degree of sonoporation. Sonovue microbubbles were introduced to passively regulate microbubble-to-cell distance and number, and bubble size. 1MHz ultrasound with 10-cycle pulse duration and 0.6MPa peak negative pressure were applied to trigger the inertial collapse of microbubbles. Our data revealed the impact of non-acoustic parameters on the heterogeneity of sonoporation. (i) The localized collapse of relatively small bubbles (diameter, D <5.5μm) led to predictable sonoporation, the degree of which depended on the bubble-to-cell distance (d). No sonoporation was observed when d/D >1, whereas reversible sonoporation occurred when d/D <1. (ii) Large bubbles (D >5.5μm) exhibited translational movement over large distances, resulting in unpredictable sonoporation. Translation towards the cell surface led to variable reversible sonoporation or irreversible sonoporation, and translation away from the cell caused either no or reversible sonoporation. (iii) The number of bubbles correlated positively with the degree of sonoporation when D <5.5μm and d/D <1. Localized collapse of two to three bubbles mainly resulted in reversible sonoporation, whereas irreversible sonoporation was more likely following the collapse of four or more bubbles. These findings offer useful insight into the relationship between non-acoustic parameters and the degree of sonoporation.

ce:section.ce:para: Sonoporation refers to the process in which the interaction of low-intensity pulsed ultrasound with microbubbles transiently perforates the plasma membrane and promotes cellular uptake of local impermeable macromolecules [1–3]. Non-invasive spatiotemporal control and low cost make this physical delivery approach promising for clinical drug delivery and gene therapy [4–6].

It is generally believed that sonoporation is induced by acoustic cavitation—the oscillation and/or collapse of microbubbles driven by ultrasound [7–9]. To realize the therapeutic application of sonoporation, great effort has been focused on improving the delivery efficiency by optimizing the acoustic and non-acoustic parameters of the system [10–12]. However, in previous studies, heterogeneous effects have been observed in sonicated cells following the occurrence of acoustic cavitation. In particular, the amount of internalized exogenous molecules in sonoporated cells was found to exhibit heterogeneous characteristics, which can be classified into different subpopulations (high, low, and nominal uptake) [8,13–15]. Moreover, sonoporated cells were reported to exhibit heterogeneous and complex concomitant physiological responses, such as calcium-ion transients [16,17], calcium oscillations and waves [18], and non-unitary changes in the levels of plasma membrane potential depolarization [19]. Finally, various cellular developmental effects including proliferation inhibition [20], cell-cycle arrest [21], and trends in cell fate (i.e., survival, apoptosis, and necrosis) have been observed in sonoporated cells over several hours following ultrasound exposure [1,22,23]. The reason for these heterogeneous effects has yet to be identified. Moreover, for practical application, the heterogeneity of sonoporation poses more challenges in achieving predictable delivery outcome and high delivery efficiency [24]. For instance, some sonicated cells that underwent apoptosis or necrosis are not advantageous to improving the delivery efficiency, because they are essentially the side effects of sonoporation [25]. Also, previous studies have shown that trends in cell fate were correlated with the degree of sonoporation [22,26]. Therefore, it is vital to understand the key acoustic and non-acoustic parameters that are related to the heterogeneity of sonoporation.

Early studies in this area used flow cytometry to characterize heterogeneous sonoporation on a cell-to-cell basis (i.e., continuous distribution of the internalized fluorescence intensity) [11,15,22]. It was found that the degree of sonoporation could be influenced by certain acoustic parameters including the peak negative pressure (PNP), pulse repetition frequency (PRF), and duration of the applied ultrasound, as well as non-acoustic parameters, such as the bubble-to-cell ratio [3,11,13]. A positive relationship between these acoustic parameters and the pore size (i.e., degree of sonoporation) was observed using direct methods such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM) or atomic force microscopy (AFM) [27–30]. Because it is not possible to measure detailed pre-exposure microbubble parameters relative to each individual cell, neither statistical analysis nor direct observation of post-exposure samples can directly reveal the cause of heterogeneous sonoporation. To further understand the interactions between ultrasound, microbubbles, and cells, recent studies have introduced real-time imaging to spatiotemporally monitor the activities of microbubbles with respect to sonoporation [31–34]. Although real-time imaging revealed that differences in the dynamic behavior of microbubbles corresponded to differences in their effect on intracellular uptake levels and pore size [35,36], these studies emphasized the relationship between the ultrasound parameters and dynamic bubble behavior, and did not fully clarify the impact of non-acoustic parameters on the outcomes of sonoporation. Previous studies have also revealed that the extent of sonoporation can be regulated by controlling the distance between the bubble and the cell [37]; however, the influence of other non-acoustic parameters, such as the bubble size and the bubble-to-cell number, on the degree of sonoporation has yet to be fully elucidated.

In this study, we surmised that different bubble-related and bubble–cell interaction parameters would influence the degree of sonoporation at the specific acoustic pressure at which the inertial collapse of bubbles occurs. Using a customized platform for ultrasound exposure and imaging, we observed the sonoporation dynamics at the single-cell level and quantified the exogenous molecular uptake levels to evaluate the extent of sonoporation. We then statistically analyzed the relationship between the non-acoustic parameters and the degree of sonoporation.

The experiments were performed according to the protocol illustrated in Fig. 1 . The cells were first cultured in a securely sealed chamber. A membrane-impermeable fluorescence marker was then mixed into the sample chamber to track the occurrence of sonoporation. Another cell-permeable fluorescent probe was also added to evaluate the cells’ viability post-exposure. Microbubbles, serving as cavitation nuclei, were finally introduced into the medium surrounding the cells. The sample chamber containing the cells, fluorescent probes, and microbubbles was mounted on an inverted fluorescence microscope, the imaging region of which was aligned with the region to be exposed to ultrasound. After the cells were subjected to a single-pulse ultrasound, the morphology and sonoporation dynamics of the cells within the region of view were recorded in real time for 10min with a camera. Finally, the acquired bright-field and fluorescence images were statistically analyzed to clarify how the non-acoustic parameters influence sonoporation.

HeLa cervical cancer cells (CCL-2; ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA) were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) (Hyclone, Thermo Scientific Waltham, MA, USA) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Gibco 10099; Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, USA) at 37°C and 5% CO2. To prepare the experimental samples, the cultured cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS), harvested by trypsinization (Gibco 25200, Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, USA), and seeded into Opticell chambers (155331, Thermo Scientific, USA) with 10mL of the same culture medium. After 1day of culture, a cell monolayer with approximately 50% confluency was obtained on one side of each chamber. Ten milliliters of Hank’s balanced salt solution (HBSS) (Invitrogen 14025, NY, USA) was added to the Opticell chamber to replace the used medium. Note that the cell confluency should be less than 50% (approximately 2×106 cells counted by hemocytometer) to facilitate observation and measurement.

Sonovue microbubbles (Bracco Research, Switzerland), which served as nuclei for acoustic cavitation in this study, are composed of sulfur hexafluoride gas packaged by a phospholipid monolayer shell. The diameter of these microbubbles ranges from 2 to 7μm [38]. The non-uniform size distribution allowed us to analyze the influence of bubble size on sonoporation. Prior to their use in the experiments, the microbubbles were freshly reconstituted in 5mL physiological saline solution. Approximately 3×106 bubbles were then transferred to 10mL HBSS in the Opticell chamber and brought in close proximity to the upper cell monolayer in the sample holder through buoyancy. There was approximately one bubble positioned near each cell for 70% of cells, and there were two or more bubbles surrounding the remaining cells. The random location distribution of microbubbles enabled us to investigate the effect of the bubble-to-cell number and distance on sonoporation.

Propidium iodide (PI; 150μM;) (P3566; Invitrogen) was used as a fluorescence marker to track sonoporation in the cells in the Opticell chamber. PI is normally excluded from the plasma membrane but can permeate the damaged membranes of sonoporated cells. After internalization, PI binds to RNA and DNA in the cytoplasm and nucleus, and forms a complex that fluoresces with a maximum at 610nm (excitation maximum: 539nm). The fluorescence intensity is related to the amount of internalized PI; thus, we can estimate the degree of sonoporation according to the fluorescence intensity of the intracellular PI-RNA and PI-DNA complexes [17].

In this work, a single-element flat transducer (6mm in diameter) (Advanced Devices, Wakefield, MA) was driven by a sinusoidal signal with a 1-MHz center frequency and 10-cycle pulse duration, which was generated by an arbitrary waveform generator (33120A, Agilent Technologies, USA) and amplified by a 50-dB RF amplifier (E&I 2100L; Electronics & Innovation Ltd., USA).

The peak negative pressure at different output voltages of the waveform generator was measured by a capsule hydrophone (HGL-0200, ONDA, Sunnyvale, CA, USA). The 1D acoustic pressure profiles were first measured in the direction of sound propagation. The hydrophone was placed 2mm from the center of the transducer and then axially moved in the direction of sound propagation by a three-axis motion stage (ASTS-01, ONDA Corporation). In Fig. 2 , the change in peak negative pressure is shown as a function of distance from the transducer. The peak negative pressure changed nonlinearly in the range of 2–6mm (Fig. 2C), which approximately corresponds to the theoretical distance of the acoustic near field (D T 2/4λ ≈6.42mm, where D T is the diameter of the transducer, and λ is the wavelength (≈1400μm)). In the far field (beyond a distance of 6mm), the peak negative pressure decreased approximately linearly with increasing distance (Fig. 2C). We intentionally chose a location 8mm from the transducer as the exposed region, to avoid significant variation in the peak negative pressure in the near field. Using the measurement method reported in reference [19], the 2D profile of the peak negative pressure at a distance of 8mm from the transducer was also mapped (Fig. 2D). In the field of view of the microscope (150μm×150μm), the peak negative pressure was relatively uniform. To induce sonoporation, a pressure of 0.6MPa was chosen because it is sufficiently intense to trigger inertial collapse of the microbubbles (inertial cavitation) [39,40].

A customized platform shown in Fig. 2A was fabricated to enable ultrasound exposure and observation of the sonoporated cells. A polystyrene quadrilateral framework (50mm×80mm×15mm) was glued to the rim of the Opticell chamber with cyanoacrylate adhesive, forming a container filled with water to couple the ultrasound pulse to the cells (Fig. 2B). The chamber was placed on the working stage of the inverted fluorescence microscope (Eclipse Ti-s, Nikon Instrument, Japan) to observe the sonoporation dynamics. The transducer, which was installed on a manual 3D positioning device, was immersed in water at 45° to avoid disturbing the illumination for the bright-field microscope images. To ensure the incident surface of the transducer was kept at a distance of 8mm from the exposed region, the center of the transducer was first placed in contact with the exposed region of the sample holder, which was aligned with the microscope’s field of view. The horizontal and vertical locations of the transducer were then adjusted 5.70mm upward and to the left, respectively, using a 3D positioning device.

We note the container used in this work was different from that used in our previous studies [19]. Our previous container design contained three barriers in the fluorescence transmission path: a 3-mm-thick container base, a 75-μm-thick polystyrene membrane of the Opticell chamber, and water. This caused considerable reflection and scattering, which led to low-quality images obtained using high-magnification objectives. In the modified container used in the present study, the fluorescence transmission is influenced only by the 75-μm-thick Opticell membrane and water, which improves the image contrast.

The fate of the sonoporated cell depends on the degree of sonoporation. 15μM Calcein Blue AM (C1429, Molecular Probe, USA) was used to evaluate the cell viability after ultrasound treatment. This probe directly permeates the membrane of viable cells, and is then retained in the cells for several hours on cleavage of the AM esters by intracellular esterases. When the cell membrane is irreversibly damaged, the dye diffuses outside of the cell and no intracellular fluorescence is observed. This process labels the viable cells with 435-nm blue fluorescence upon maximum 320-nm excitation.

A 40×objective lens with a working distance of 3.6–2.8mm (S Plan Fluor ELWD, Nikon Instruments, Japan) was used for imaging. Each experiment was performed with an exposure time of 150ms and 1.4×analog gain, controlled by NIS-Elements software (Version 4.0, Nikon Instruments, Japan). Bright-field images and basal fluorescence were recorded before ultrasound treatment. From the beginning of ultrasound application, a charge-coupled device camera was used to capture images in the region of interest over a 10-min period. The first PI fluorescence image was taken 10s after ultrasound exposure and subsequent images were acquired in 30-s intervals. At the end of the 10-min observation period, a final Calcein Blue image was taken.

The bubble diameter (i.e., the maximum radial length of the bubble) and the bubble-to-cell distance, (i.e., the minimum distance between the external contour margins of the bubble and the cell) were measured using a built-in function in the NIS-Elements software. To eliminate random error, three measurements were averaged to obtain the final value.

The PI fluorescence images acquired at different times during the 10-min recording period were extracted from the acquired video. The mean fluorescence intensity of the Calcein Blue images at 10min and PI images were measured with the NIS-Elements software. The relative changes (ΔF) in the PI and Calcein Blue fluorescence at different times (Fn ) post-exposure were normalized with respect to the pre-exposure basal fluorescence (F 0) and presented as the percentage difference (ΔF =(Fn
− F 0)/F 0).

After the sonicated cells were characterized, different sonoporation types were compared based on the Student t-test and the Bonferroni correction to determine statistical significance of these types.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-acoustic parameters related to the heterogeneity of sonoporated cells
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with the negative control (Cell 1), which was not exposed to ultrasound (Fig. 3 A), the cells that were subjected to our ultrasound and microbubble exposure protocol exhibited heterogeneous sonoporation. This is apparent in four typical cells with different intracellular uptake levels (Cells 2–5 in Fig. 3B–E). Time-lapse of the relative PI fluorescence of these cells over a 520-s period is depicted in Fig. 3F. It can be seen that Cells 3–5 exhibited a rapid increase in PI uptake within the first 10 s after ultrasound exposure, indicating the occurrence of sonoporation. In contrast, no change is evident in Cell 2 compared with the negative control (Cell 1). Moreover, Cells 3–5 display different trends in the increase of intracellular PI fluorescence over time. PI uptake in Cells 3 and 4 rapidly increased within 70s after ultrasound exposure, and gradually reached a stable level thereafter. This course suggests that PI could not sustainably diffuse into Cell 3 and Cell 4 due to the resealing of the perforated membrane. Moreover, these two cells exhibited different PI uptake levels relative to that in the negative control at 520s post-exposure (approximately 0.79 and 5.35-fold higher than the baseline level, respectively). However, PI uptake into Cell 5 continuously increased throughout a long period (400s), reaching an approximately 12-fold higher than the baseline level at 520s. This is indicative of unsuccessful repair of the perforated membrane.

The different PI uptake characteristics of Cells 2–5 suggest that these cells underwent different degrees of sonoporation. Moreover, the post-exposure fate of the cells varied. The corresponding results from the Calcein Blue analysis are shown in Fig. 3B–E. The viability of Cells 2–4 was maintained because the Calcein Blue level in these cells was similar to that in the negative control (Cell 1). In contrast, Cell 5 lost its viability, as indicated by the leakage of Calcein Blue from the cytosol.

These cells were exposed to the same ultrasound conditions (PNP, and 10-μs duration) but different non-acoustic parameters (bubble size, and bubble-to-cell distance and number). Cells 2, 3, and 5 neighbored just one bubble; the bubble diameters (D) were 3.25, 4.41, and 5.62μm, respectively (bright-field images at 0 s in Fig. 3B, C, and E). Cell 4 was adjacent to 2 bubbles with diameters of 5.31 and 3.61μm (Fig. 3B). The distances (d) of the bubbles from Cells 2, 3, and 5 were 4.56, 1.23, and 3.40μm, respectively. Cell 4 was positioned 0.50μm from the first bubble and 0.63μm away from the second bubble. It is therefore likely that the observed heterogeneous sonoporation is attributable to variations in these non-acoustic parameters.

To gain further insight into the characteristics of heterogeneous sonoporation, according to the relative intracellular PI uptake level at 10min post-exposure, more than 160 sonicated cells were quantitatively and statistically analyzed, and categorized into different types (the number of the samples of each type was greater than 35), as shown in Fig. 4 A. The viability analysis of these cells is shown in Fig. 4B.

As can be seen in the first two box-whisker plots in Fig. 4A, type I cells exhibited no distinct change in the relative PI uptake level compared with the negative control and did not experience sonoporation. The Calcein Blue fluorescence intensity in these cells was similar to the control, indicating their normal viability (first two box-whisker plots in Fig. 4B). A representative example of this type of behavior is Cell 2 in Fig. 3B.

The relative PI uptake levels in type II sonoporated cells, as shown in the third and fourth box-whisker plots in Fig. 4A, are statistically different (p <0.001) from the uptake levels in the control and type I cells. As confirmed by the viability assay in Fig. 4B, type II cells retained their activity at 10min post-exposure. Thus, these cells underwent reversible sonoporation. However, it is noteworthy that type II cells, despite remaining viable through successful membrane resealing, experience different degrees of sonoporation (as inferred from the statistically different (p <0.001) relative PI uptake levels). We further classified type II cells into two sub-groups: sub-group II-1 and sub-group II-2, which exhibited mild and moderate PI uptake (less than twofold and approximately two- to eightfold higher than the baseline level), respectively. Cells 3 and 4 in Fig. 3C and D exemplify cells in sub-group II-1 and sub-group II-2 sonoporation.

The relative PI uptake in type III sonoporated cells, shown in the last box-whisker plot in Fig. 4A, was very high—more than eight times higher than that in the control—and statistically different from that in each sub-group of the type II sonoporated cells (p <0.001). Moreover, the Calcein Blue assay in Fig. 4B showed that type III sonoporated cells were nonviable and therefore underwent irreversible sonoporation. Cell 5 in Fig. 3E belongs to this type of sonoporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversible sonoporation induced by the localized collapse of small bubbles
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the relationship between the degree of sonoporation and the bubble size, we quantitatively analyzed the effect of the bubble diameter on intracellular PI uptake for fixed bubble-to-cell distances. The results for four different bubble-to-cell distances of approximately 0, 1.5, 3, and 4.5μm are presented in Fig. 5 A–D, respectively. The reason for varying this parameter is that the spatial location of the bubble relative to the cell is an important non-acoustic factor that influences the outcome of sonoporation. From the data in Fig. 5, the degree of sonoporation was found to correlate with the bubble diameter for each of the four bubble-to-cell distances studied. A bubble diameter of approximately 5.5μm can be seen to be a critical size (i.e., bubbles with greater or smaller diameters than this experience different sonoporation behaviors). In this study, bubbles smaller than 5.5μm in diameter are defined as “small bubbles” and those larger than 5.5μm in diameter are defined as “large bubbles”.

In the case of small bubbles (Fig. 5A–D), the sonicated cells exhibited no and low PI uptake, characteristic of type I or II sonoporation, respectively. Moreover, at each bubble-to-cell distance, the PI uptake levels slightly increased with increasing bubble diameter. As shown in Fig. 5A, with increasing bubble diameter, the intracellular PI uptake increased significantly from mild to moderate levels, corresponding to sub-group II-1 and sub-group II-2 sonoporation, respectively.

The influence of the dynamic behavior of bubbles on sonoporation was also investigated. A representative example of the behavior of a small bubble is given in Supplementary Video 1, which corresponds to Cell 3 in Fig. 3C. As can be noted, after ultrasound exposure, this small bubble synchronously exhibited rapidly localized collapse (inertial cavitation) with limited translation, leading to localized PI uptake characterized by type II sonoporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unpredictable sonoporation caused by the translation of large bubbles
ce:section.ce:para: For the large bubbles studied, variable and unpredictable PI uptake was observed for all bubble-to-cell distances, as illustrated in Fig. 5, suggesting that large-bubble-induced sonoporation is independent of the bubble-to-cell distance. This result is further demonstrated in Supplementary Videos 2 and 3. Unlike the case for small bubbles (Supplementary Video 1), the large bubbles gained sufficient momentum as a result of ultrasound exposure to rapidly move over large distances before collapsing. Moreover, the direction of this translational movement appears to be related to the degree of sonoporation. For Cells 2′ and 5 in Videos 3 and 2, the bubble moved rapidly across the surface of the cell membrane leading to moderate and high PI delivery in these two cells, respectively. The Calcein Blue assay in Figs. S1 and 3E indicated that they remained viable and became nonviable, characteristic of type II (sub-group II-2) and type III sonoporation, respectively. However, for Cell 1′ in Supplemental Video 3, the bubble moved in the opposite direction away from the cell. This caused low PI uptake in Cell 1′, which remained viable by Calcein Blue assay (Fig. S1) and experienced type II (sub-group II-1) sonoporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dependence of small-bubble-induced sonoporation on the bubble-to-cell distance
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis in the previous sections demonstrated that small bubbles can be effective for obtaining predictably reversible sonoporation (type II), which is desirable in biomedical applications. In this section, we determine the relationship between sonoporation and the bubble-to-cell distance (d). The results are shown in Fig. 6 A–C, where d was varied for fixed bubble diameters of approximately 2.5, 3.5, and 4.5μm, respectively. We found that the localized collapse of small bubbles triggered sonoporation only within a certain range of bubble-to-cell distances. For bubble diameters of 2.5, 3.5, and 4.5μm, low PI uptake characteristics of type II reversible sonoporation was found within bubble-to-cell distance ranges of about 2.5, 3.5, and 4.5μm, respectively; no PI uptake was observed outside of these ranges. The intracellular PI uptake level in these cases was negatively correlated with the bubble-to-cell distance. These results demonstrate that sonoporation is dependent on the bubble-to-cell distance, and that there is a threshold distance for triggering sonoporation, which is positively correlated with the diameter of the bubble.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation between the number of bubbles and the degree of sonoporation
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the number of bubbles on sonoporation was studied under the following conditions based on the conclusions detailed in the previous section: (i) the bubble diameter was less than 5.5μm and (ii) the bubble was closer to the cell than the threshold distance. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . When a single bubble was located adjacent to the cell, sonoporation was predominantly (98%) type II (sub-group II-1) in more than 40 experiments. For cells in the proximity of two bubbles, 51% and 42% of more than 30 sonicated cells exhibited the characteristics of type II sonoporation (sub-groups II-1 and II-2, respectively), and about 5% underwent type III sonoporation. When three bubbles were situated near the cell, the percentage of type II (sub-group II-2) and type III sonoporation became notably higher (64% and 17%, respectively). With a further increase of the number of bubbles to between 4 and 6, the percentage of type III sonoporation was significantly increased to about 98%. These results suggest that the heterogeneity of sonoporation is related to the number of bubbles adjacent to the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effective spatial range (d/D <1) for triggering sonoporation
ce:section.ce:para: As detailed in the previous section, small bubbles can induce predictable sonoporation although the occurrence and degree of sonoporation strongly depend on the distance between the bubble and the cell, and the diameter of the collapsing bubble. We here defined a normalized factor d/D to further illustrate the comprehensive influence of the bubble-to-cell distance and the bubble size on sonoporation. The results, which were obtained by reorganizing the experimental data presented in the previous section, are shown in Fig. 8 A.

For D <5.5μm, the degree of sonoporation depends on the normalized factor d/D, and intracellular PI uptake levels approximately reduced with increasing d/D. Type II (sub-groups II-1 and II-2) reversible sonoporation (i.e., relatively low PI uptake) was observed when d/D <1, whereas type I behavior (i.e., no PI uptake) occurred when d/D >1. These findings suggest that the ultrasound conditions alone do not determine the occurrence or degree of sonoporation, and that the influences of microjets or shockwaves produced by the collapsing bubbles are effective only within the spatial range of d/D <1. Moreover, using different contact condition of the bubbles to cells, previous studies also qualitatively found that cells could be perforated just in the range of microjets [41].

However, when d/D <1, the sonicated cells, which all underwent type II sonoporation, exhibited slightly different intracellular PI uptake levels even for the same d/D value. This might be because of differences in the locations of the microbubbles relative to the cells, which have irregular morphologies. When a microbubble was located around the lamellipodia of a cell, its rapid collapse generated a localized impact on a very small area of the cell surface, resulting in relatively low PI uptake. However, when a microbubble was located around the cell body, its collapse affected a larger area of the cell’s surface, leading to relatively high PI uptake. Thus, even when the bubble-to-cell distance and acoustic parameters are held constant, differences in the locations of the microbubbles around the cell boundary may cause slight differences in the intracellular PI uptake.

For large bubbles, the relationship between the degree of sonoporation and d/D is shown in Fig. 8B. It can be seen that different types of sonoporation behavior (type I–III) occurred randomly and unpredictably even for d/D <1. This is attributed to ultrasound-initiated translation of large bubbles. The following would explain why large bubbles exhibit distinct translation movement when exposure to ultrasound.

Translation of bubbles results from the work done by the primary acoustic radiation force (F arf), which is dependent on the acoustic parameters (acoustic frequency, PRF and pressure) as well as the properties of the bubbles (shell viscosity and stiffness, bubble size) [42,43]. The calculation of F arf is detailed in the Supplementary material and the results are shown in Fig. S2. We note that because the acoustic conditions in the present study are constant (0.6MPa peak negative pressure, 10μs duration), the acoustic radiation force on the bubble correlates with the bubble diameter. A nonlinear dependency of F arf on D can be observed in Fig. S2. At bubble diameters of 2.5, 5.0, 5.5, and 6.5μm, F arf is approximately 1.95, 17.89, 26.87, and 67.33nN, respectively. Thus, large bubbles are more strongly influenced by the acoustic radiation force, which causes them to undergo pronounced translation leading to unpredictable sonoporation.

Characterizing the relationship between the non-acoustic parameters and sonoporation is important for optimizing sonoporation for biomedical applications. This investigation here renders a summative model about how the non-acoustic factors affect the degree of sonoporation, which is illustrated in Fig. 9 .

The localized collapse of small bubbles generates predictable sonoporation, the degree of which correlates with the bubble-to-cell distance. No sonoporation was observed when d/D >1, whereas type II reversible sonoporation occurred when d/D <1. The dependence of type II sonoporation on the bubble-to-cell distance suggests that targeted bubbles that bind to cells and reduce the spatial distance from cells would be the most suitable for improving the delivery efficiency [35,44]. This conclusion was also drawn in previous studies comparing targeted and non-targeted bubbles [45]. However, for large bubbles, ultrasound-induced translation motion is not necessarily productive for intracellular delivery. This finding can also be regarded as a supplement of the translation movement of bubbles induced by long pulses duration ultrasound that has been studied [39]. Designing and obtaining uniform microbubbles is worth investigating to eliminate unpredictable sonoporation in drug delivery.

Compared with the case of a single cavitation bubble acting on the cell, the action of two or three collapsing bubbles increases the number of perforations in the cell membrane, resulting in more type II (sub-group II-2) sonoporation, which may improve the diffusion efficiency of macromolecules into cells [10]. This results were consistent with earlier findings by others that showed the dependence of the cell membrane perforation on the number of bubbles adjacent to each cell [41]. According to our previous reports [19], we suggest that type II (sub-group II-2) sonoporated cells, despite remaining viable through membrane resealing, may exhibit sustained membrane depolarization and subsequently undergo abnormal development in the long term, such as proliferation disorder or apoptosis. Finally, more than four collapsing bubbles near a cell readily induced irreversible sonoporation, which is not useful for improving the delivery efficiency. These findings suggest that the number of bubbles should be carefully controlled to achieve the desired delivery outcome.

Sonoporation is a complex biophysical process that is influenced by a variety of acoustic and non-acoustic parameters. In this work, we investigated the effect of non-acoustic parameters on the heterogeneity of sonoporation at the single-cell level. Our findings would be a supplement for the previous studies on the relationship between different acoustic parameters or bubble activities and the outcome of sonoporation, and could help deepen our understanding of the interaction between ultrasound, microbubbles, and cells. Moreover, we expect this study can further provide insight into the biophysical mechanisms involved in sonoporation and useful guidance for the application of sonoporation in drug delivery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81201098, No. 81471667), Program of Medicine and Engineering Cross Fund of Shanghai Jiao Tong University – China (YG2015ZD09, YG2013MS02), and University of Waterloo Startup Funds (203873).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2015.12.001.
